result: The first time I felt the pull of an empty page, I was sitting in a cramped café, the scent of coffee mixing with the murmur of strangers. There was a notebook in front of me, its lines unblemished, waiting. I placed the pen against the paper and felt a sudden hush, as if the world outside had paused to listen. In that moment, words didn’t have to be perfect; they simply needed to exist. I wrote about a rainstorm that turned the street into a river, about a child chasing a kite that kept slipping beyond reach, about the way a simple smile could alter the course of an ordinary day. The act of putting thoughts into sentences felt like unlocking a door to a room I never knew I owned. Over the years, that room has grown—walls lined with stories, shelves packed with images, and a floor that wobbles under the weight of curiosity. Writing freely isn’t just about letting ideas spill; it’s about trusting the process, accepting mistakes as stepping stones, and allowing the voice inside to whisper, then shout, then sing. It reminds me that language is a living thing, capable of bending, stretching, and reinventing itself with each new sentence. So whenever the page feels blank, I remind myself that the blankness is simply a canvas, and the pen is ready to paint whatever imagination conjures. Every story begins with a single line, and that line can lead you anywhere you truly dare to imagine.
